original_text:
### Editorial / Style Rules
- **Abbreviation "DM":** After first mention of "dermatomyositis," use "DM" in running copy to avoid repetition. **Do NOT use "DM" in headlines, headings, or subheads** — always the full word there (clarity, accessibility, and SEO on web). First-mention pattern: `dermatomyositis (DM)`.
  - Because sections are assembled later: **[GENERAL] every standalone section must establish `dermatomyositis (DM)` at its own first mention** unless the assembly brief states the abbreviation is established upstream.
- **Inclusive language (patient materials):** say "someone with dermatomyositis" / "people with dermatomyositis" — **not "patients."** ("Patients" is acceptable only in HCP materials.) Use **"care team"** (not "staff") for the network of patients, HCPs, and support systems.
- **Numbers:** spell out zero–nine; numerals for 10+. **Exceptions — always numerals: percentages, ages, ratios.** Percentages use the % sign (e.g., 74%).
- **Data tone:** when citing clinical data, avoid negative or fear-based language; use an empathetic, supportive, trust-building tone without oversimplifying. Example pattern: "Participants in the VALOR study taking LISRAYA had nearly 50% more improvement in their DM symptoms after one year compared to placebo."
- **Punctuation:** Oxford/serial comma always in series of 3+.

quotes: every standalone section must establish `dermatomyositis (DM)` at its own first mention
After first mention of "dermatomyositis," use "DM" in running copy to avoid repetition. Do NOT use "DM" in headlines, headings, or subheads — always the full word there
every standalone section must establish `dermatomyositis (DM)` at its own first mention

rules.intent: Ensures clarity, accessibility, and SEO across dynamic or standalone section assemblies.
rules.rule_text: Establish the abbreviation 'DM' on its first mention in running copy as 'dermatomyositis (DM)' within every standalone section. Do not use 'DM' in headlines, headings, or subheads; always use the full word there.

quotes: say "someone with dermatomyositis" / "people with dermatomyositis" — not "patients."

Inclusive language (patient materials): say "someone with dermatomyositis" / "people with dermatomyositis" — not "patients." ("Patients" is acceptable only in HCP materials.) Use "care team" (not "staff") for the network

rules.intent: Enforce human-centric, patient-first inclusive language in brand copy.
rules.rule_text: In patient-facing materials, refer to the audience as 'someone with dermatomyositis' or 'people with dermatomyositis' rather than 'patients'. Refer to their network as 'care team' instead of 'staff'.

quotes: Numbers: spell out zero–nine; numerals for 10+. Exceptions — always numerals: percentages, ages, ratios. Percentages use the % sign (e.g., 74%).

rules.intent: Standardize numerical styling for clinical accuracy and readability.
rules.rule_text: Spell out numbers zero through nine, and use numerals for numbers 10 and above. Always use numerals for percentages (using the '%' symbol), ages, and ratios.

quotes: Data tone: when citing clinical data, avoid negative or fear-based language; use an empathetic, supportive, trust-building tone without oversimplifying.

rules.intent: Maintain clinical integrity while prioritizing an empathetic patient relationship.
rules.rule_text: When citing clinical data, do not use fear-based or negative language. Adopt an empathetic, supportive, and trust-building tone without oversimplifying the data.

quotes: Punctuation: Oxford/serial comma always in series of 3+.

rules.intent: Maintain rigorous editorial consistency and resolve list-group ambiguity.
rules.rule_text: Always use the Oxford (serial) comma in list series containing three or more items.
